messages.full_response:
VERDICT: FAKE

EXPLANATION:
- The claim "Drinking bleach cures COVID-19" is thoroughly debunked by multiple credible sources, including the World Health Organization (WHO), the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), and reputable health websites such as Healthline.
- These sources explicitly state that drinking bleach does not cure or prevent COVID-19 and can lead to severe harm, disability, and death due to its poisonous nature.
- The evidence gathered from various sources, including news articles, fact-checking websites, and academic reports, consistently confirms that the claim is a baseless rumor with no credible backing.
- Internet search results, including articles from PMC, medRxiv, CIDRAP, Healthline, and Public Health Post, all conclude that drinking bleach is not a treatment for COVID-19 and is extremely dangerous.
- The CDC and other health authorities have warned against this practice, and there is no scientific evidence supporting its use.
- The consistency of the evidence from multiple sources confirms that the claim is false, and the potential harm caused by drinking bleach makes it a significant public health concern.

CONFIDENCE: HIGH

SOURCES USED:
- https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10321604/
- https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2020.12.11.20246694v3.full-text
- https://www.cidrap.umn.edu/covid-19/report-no-evidence-bleach-consumed-cure-covid-19-during-pandemic
- https://www.healthline.com/health/drinking-bleach
- https://publichealthpost.org/health-equity/dont-drink-the-bleach-understanding-conspiracy-theories-and-misinformation/

# Report: No evidence bleach consumed to cure COVID-19 during pandemic

A new report dispels accounts that Americans drank bleach to cure or prevent COVID-19, a practice even the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) warned against during the early months of the pandemic. The report was published yesterday in PLOS One.

Fellow   Adna Jaganjac

 Symbol of the misinformation available on the internet about the coronavirus pandemic

Read Time: 3 minutes

Published:

 

What is strangest or most dangerous piece of misinformation you’ve encountered on social media? Here’s one: the claim that drinking bleach cures COVID-19.

messages.full_response:
VERDICT: REAL

EXPLANATION: 
- The claim "Donald Trump is alive" is supported by multiple sources, including recent news articles, official biographies, and government websites.
- Sources such as Wikipedia, The White House, CNN, PBS, Euronews, Simple English Wikipedia, and Ballotpedia confirm that Donald Trump is alive and has served as the 45th and 47th President of the United States.
- The Legacy website, which provides obituaries and funeral services, does not have a listing for Donald Trump, further supporting the conclusion that he is alive.
- Donald Trump has also addressed viral rumors of his death, stating he was unaware of them, and has been seen in public and active on social media.
- The overall evidence gathered, including internet search results and news articles, confirms that Donald Trump is alive.

CONFIDENCE: HIGH

SOURCES USED:
- https://people.com/donald-trump-responds-health-speculation-death-rumors-11801702
- https://www.britannica.com/biography/Donald-Trump
- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dHhOBBuPGqE
- https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/watch-trump-calls-online-rumors-of-his-death-crazy-and-based-on-fake-news
- https://www.cnn.com/politics/president-donald-trump-47

sources.title: Donald Trump Reacts to Viral Death Rumors on Live TV - People.com
sources.content:
President Donald Trump has offered his first in-person comments about the viral rumors that his health is failing.

During a press conference on Tuesday, Sept. 2, Trump, 79, was asked by a reporter whether he was aware that many social media users thought he died last week.

"No," he replied. "Really? I didn't see that."
